Music So clearly the live action fire, you see a real pitch, you see a pitch deck. It's not as packaged as the package presentation, but you see what was really working. Now this is some more live action fire. I sit on the intake committee of a family office, about an $80 million fund family office. Whenever they get a deal that they're interested in, they send it over to me and I look at it. And I say, guys, I like it. And then if we're interested, they bring in the company and they pitch us. So I see pitches all the time. I sit on the intake committee at a hedge fund. Control is about $100 million of investment capital. And I probably know $5 or $6 billion of capital out there. So when people come to me, I really get deals financed. That's what I do. Being a book writer is not my, you know, or speaking on stage, as you can see is not my day-to-day grind, getting deals financed. So I thought I would hear from you a little about your deal and then reflect back to you maybe a different way of pitching it, the way I would like to be pitched it. So tell me a little bit about yourself and your company. Thank you for choosing me. My name is William Martin. The company is United Minds out of Oro Valley, Arizona. It's a publicly traded company with the StockSymbol UNMN. The business is about eight years old. It's been public for about two years now. It's a real deal and it's got a pretty interesting history. In the mid-1800s, it was owned by Samuel Colt from Colt Firearms. And at the time, it was the largest producing silver mine in the world. And it was last in production in the early 70s. But we're trying to raise two to four million dollars right now to put it back into production. There are approximately three to five hundred thousand ounces of silver sitting on top of the ground in mine dump tailings that they didn't have the technology to process and extract the silver at the time. And plus, silver was too cheap. Now that it's up around thirty dollars an ounce, there is much more, makes a lot more sense. And the people behind it have combined about two hundred years of mining experience. Let me ask you this. What is your business model? The business model is to raise the money, put it back into production and put drills into the ground to further explore. There's three to five billion dollars in furred, but we need to get in there and prove it. Alright. What's the problem? What's the problem? The problem is that probably we're too small for the biggies. And it's been difficult to get out and get in front of the right people. And as you know, there's millions, there's a million deals out there. So what problem does the company solve though? Right? So why don't other people do this? Why is this hard to do? Raising money is... So no, no, the company, the actual extraction. Well, it's not necessarily all that hard to do, but it takes money. Okay, so what's the use of funds when you raise the money? The first money will go into extracting the silver that's in the mine dump tailings that's sitting above ground as we speak. And then, so what's the gross margin? What are the economics? So you spend a million dollars and what happens? Well, the return right off the million dollars should return somewhere between nine and fifteen million dollars, just from the silver that's in the mine dump tailings. And that will give more than enough money to get the drills into the ground to prove out the reserves, the inferred reserves. Got it. Alright, so let me reflect back a way that I think this could be done very powerfully. So when I ask someone, what's your business model? This is what I'm looking for, especially in this situation. What's your business model? Many people think, you say, what's your business model? Oh, well, we buy a widget, we add some value to it, we mark it up, we put a label on it and we sell it. So our business model is that we are a value-added reseller. That's how we make our margin. That's not a business model. If you go to Harvard or Yale and you take a class with Michael Porter on business models, this is what he'll say. What do you do yourself and what do you outsource? What do you not do? First of all, okay? Second, who are your customers? And which customers in your market will let you profit the most from them? Somebody should write this down because I do not have a slide on this. So what do you do and what do you not do? Who are your target customers and who will let you profit the most from them? How do you get into the sales channel? How do you go to market? How do you get in the channel? How do you capture value as part of the value that you provide? So how do you charge? How do you capture value as part of the value you provide? And how do you defend your revenue streams from other people who want those same revenues? So let's feed your deal through that model. So what is it you do specifically versus what do you outsource? Specifically, we mine the metal out of the ore out of the ground and then we can actually process it right on the ground. We are actually, the facility is fully permitted to a leech field to extract the metals into a concentrate and then we'll outsource the smelting of the concentrate. What's hard about mining? Well, digging holes and the way this is done, it's not like the mines are real deep and they can collapse a lot of surface mining and following veins down. Okay, alright. So just the physical, so this is not buying servers and making Facebook. This is physical interaction with the earth and that's hard. Correct. Alright, and dangerous. Well, not so much, not like it used to be. Okay, so who are the customers? Because I think this is very important, right? We heard a pitch here, but we didn't hear anything about the customers yet. So who are the customers and not only who are the customers, but which customers will let us profit the most? Well, the customer is anyone who believes in silver and thinks that the price of silver is going to rise. So we can't have customers who are anyone. Okay. I mean, at this point, really, the customers were looking for some investors that are able to write a check, you know, for a substantial... So the customers are investors. Yes. Alright. Tell me a little bit about this customer, how big is the check? Because we might find some check riders right here in a few minutes. I mean, in reality, it would be nice to have like, you know, one or two write a check for like two to four million, but I guess, you know, there's a few ways to do this. The company, the lowest amount of stock that the company has ever sold publicly was, or is a dollar 25 a share. Right now, it's trading around 50 cents. It came out at $5, but since it's not in production, you know, it gets beaten down and what have you, there's only... It's got one of the lowest, probably the lowest float of any public company out there. There's only like 15 million shares that are in circulation. Okay. How do you get in the channel? How do you go out and find the investors? Right? So, well, let's go back to the investors, but who's the perfect? Right? Because there's... anybody has a deal in here? There's a thousand investors within a hundred miles of here. Alright? And investors... let's pause on this. Investors segment themselves into risk reward buckets. Right? So, I had a deal a few months ago, a guaranteed 20% return. Guaranteed, because a friend of mine had invested in a software company and needed some more money, but his fund was at their limit for an individual investment. So, he says, if you bring in a million dollars from one of your buddies, we'll guarantee it with our assets. Guaranteed 20% return. So, I go to some of my venture buddies and I say, I got an easy one for you. Guaranteed 20% return by the assets of a well-known hedge fund. They say, oh, that's great. 20% return is awesome. But we're a venture group. We try to get 3 to 5X. We try to get a 10X on our money. Even if we get this 20%, our investors will be mad at us for returning 20%. That's not what we're in the business of getting. So, as great as this is, you're wasting your time with us. We can't take a 20% return. We've heard that before. Yeah, and now the other thing you'll hear, so I go to other guys. Now, guys, I guarantee you a 20% return by the assets of a hedge fund. It's real. Or we'd love to get a 20% return. But in our business, we dream of getting 10s. We dream of hitting a 10 bogey. We dream of getting a 10% annual return. And we know in reality we only get 7s for what we know. So, a 20% would make our investors and our partners very, very uncomfortable. Because it's completely outside of what we think is achievable. So, investors have a very specific risk-reward appetite. And so, I said there's a thousand investors within 100 miles of here. I could introduce you to 200 of them. But within 20 seconds of meeting them, they're going to say, we don't have a bucket for that. It's not in our risk return profile. So, now we've got to think about, and we may not be able to do it here, who will let us profit? Who wants to? Who has a bucket for this? Who has this risk return appetite? So, on here, some people want to get very, very certain 10% yield. And on the other side of the book, and some people don't care about losing their money, but they want a chance to make a 10 times return. And we're somewhere in the middle. So, we've got to find, we've got to filter the market. And in any business, right, there's all kinds of customers, but who will let us profit from them? Who has a bucket for this product or this money? So, we've got to think more about, who's the perfect customer? Who's a perfect investor? Do you have any sense of that? Well, I have the perfect investor, somebody who's looking to make a nice return, but, you know, in a reasonable amount of time. Okay. I got it. Next, let's talk about how you get into the channel, how you're going out to find these investors or buyers, right? So, just a quick refresher. We've got to figure out these five things to be able to talk about this business. What you do and what you don't do, we've got that cover. Who are the ideal investors for it? Okay. What is their, what is their psychographic? And now we're talking about where do you find them? So, where's the best place to find investors for the deal? How do you get into the channel? That's an excellent question. Because it's a publicly traded company, one has to be careful about statements made, etc. So, you know, it's been word of mouth and, you know, I mean, this is an awesome opportunity here to get in front of a group like this. Okay. So, next, how do you make money in this? How do you make money? How do you capture value as part of the value you give? I don't think that was clear to all of us. So, what is the specific economic value proposition? I'm not quite sure how to answer that. All right. So, we extract something from the earth. Yes. We did something with it. Yeah. Then we outsourced the smelting and then we got some valuable mineral. Right? So, how much do we spend on doing that and what is it now worth? What is spent is minimal compared to what it's worth. What is minimal? Probably, I believe it's around 10%. So, silver is around $30 an ounce right now. So, it's, you know, $3 to $4 to get it back into a form that's marketable. So, there might be an 80% gross margin, 80 or more gross margin on the product. Correct. Okay. So, that's how we capture value. How, so if we get a lot of investors, right, and this business is going well, why would people choose us over anyone else? What unfair advantage do we have? Do we have mining licenses that nobody else has? Do we know how to sell it? What unfair advantage do we have in this market? That's an excellent question. Now, we do have 3,000 acres of mines permitted from the Bureau of Mining and the federal government. Okay. All right. So, we've got 3,000 acres and nobody else can take away because we own them. Correct. All right. So, last, let's try and figure out, the last thing we want to try and figure out here. We understand. So, let's just run through it again. All right. What's changing in this business? Let's figure out what's changing in this business. All right. What the, why there's an opportunity here, right? What's making this opportunity come to market? What's changing? What's making this opportunity come to market is the need for cash to get into production. Okay. What's changing in the market for silver? The price appears to be heading north. Okay. Price is going north. Is there anything in the technology of mining that's changing? Technology, you know, continues to get better all the time. And... Okay. I'm going to try and pitch this deal to you with what I know. All right. So, let me have you take a seat back here. Thank you. So, you can watch it. Thank you very much. That's awesome. That's for you. Thank you. I love it. All right. All right. Thank you very much for sharing that. My tiny pea brain is moving up 100 miles an hour trying to figure this deal out. That's that noise you hear. I want to tell you a little bit about our business. We're a mining company. We do something very difficult. In today's world, you hear about lots of businesses and they're on the front page of Wired Magazine and the New York Times and the Wall Street Journal and they buy servers and they take them to a little server farm and they got lots of little 24-year-olds and 19-year-olds coding away. They do engineering and they release a product like Groupon. It's all happens in air conditioned rooms in up in Silicon Valley. We're a different kind of business. We work in the physical world. We do something very hard. We take heavy machinery and we apply it against the earth and against the will of the earth. We extract massive amounts of raw product, of raw material. It's just as hard as that is, challenging the will of planet earth. Just knowing where to do this is an information feat beyond the scope of even an hour or two hours or weeks of description. You need PhDs in mineralogy and analyzing satellite photos and you need to have... We can't be a 24-year-old. We don't have a single 24-year-old in our company except the guy who washes the dishes. Okay? This is guys and if a guy comes in and he says, in the server business Groupon, they get a 50-year-old guy in that business. He's a senior citizen. They wonder what he's doing there. In our business, guys 50 years old, we say, oh, you're just getting started. I'm not going to go dig anywhere. I'm not going to go dig anywhere that somebody who's not 55 to 65 years old has told me to dig because they need to have had 40 years in this business, but I won't trust millions of dollars of equipment and men and people and lives to go challenge the will of the planet, to go extract raw material. But once we've done all this, it's incredibly hard work. We extract this material and then we sort it all out and then we send it to a smelter. He returns the raw material to us and we have this incredibly rare and valuable element and the way we do it because we've been doing it for a long time and we understand the technology and we've got experienced people. The way we do this very difficult thing, we get 80 to 90% gross margin on this effort. So if we do it and we do it as well as it can be done, we get a 90% gross margin, but on our worst day, we get a 70% gross margin. It's a very, very profitable business, very profitable. But things are changing in our market. If you don't understand these changes, you would not want to participate because gold and silver and copper and commodities is a business that goes up and it goes down. And if you think it's going up, when it's going down, you're hosed and we would never want anybody to work with us to misunderstand what's happening in this market. So you've got to understand three drivers of this business today. One is the value of silver is trending up. And it has been trending and the experts who track commodities are telling us it's got another four or five years of trending. And they're wrong. You could look at their 50 years of prediction and they're not wrong about this stuff. The models are very good. So you're going to see another four or five years of lift in the market. Now we don't know exactly what day it's going to turn or when the lift is going to go shallow, but it's not going to turn around tomorrow. These markets are stable. They're not mercurial. They don't change on a dime. In fact, you have a war in Iran. Israel drops the super bomb on Iran and the price goes up, which hopefully will happen. So that's one thing is you can look at the trend lines and the models and the trend line is up. The second thing is happening is because of the economic downturn, more people are moving into hard commodities with their money. They want that as a protective element. So you're seeing a social change in this business where before it just used to be for expert traders. But now man on the street, your average guy, you have financial managers recommending these kinds of commodities as a safe haven. So you've got a social change and there's also a very strong technological change. A very strong technological change happening in this business. We just know how to mine better. The technology is getting better. We get more extraction for less effort and more money. So if you have a company that knows how to apply the new technologies, you can have a significant advantage. So the prices are trending up and they will trend up for a while. The models are good. You have to understand that. Number two, you understand the social change, which people are getting, seeing this more as a safe haven is becoming more acceptable. And three, the technologies are getting better. This is opening a window of opportunity that we're stepping through. Now other people do this. Other people do this and other people do this well. There are 10 companies in the market that do what we do. They own thousands of acres. They apply their force of will against the earth with equipment. They have very seasoned professionals and they extract this stuff from the ground and they use outsourced smelters and they create incredibly valuable commodity and they sell it in the market. But we do something that's very special. We know where to dig better than anyone else. That's where we invested a lot of our money. We know where to dig better than anyone else. We have a team of analysis. We spend more than anybody else. You know, it's interesting. I'll just pause on this for a minute. When America and Russia were coming to power, America and Russia were coming to power, what happened is the late 30s, early 40s, America believed that microelectronics were going to be the dominant technology that would rule the political structure of the world. And Russia believed that it was metallurgy. The better rockets and engines and tanks and guns that you could make with metal that you would be able to dominate the political structure of the world. But nobody knew for sure what the outcome would be. So America chose microelectronics and we invested in that. And we created Silicon Valley and we have MIT and we have a huge amount of government and company infrastructure around microelectronics. And Russia focused their entire academic and governmental infrastructure on getting great metallurgy. Now today the United States of America buys rocket engines from Russia. Both technology and actual rocket engines. Their jet fighters do things that jet fighters built by the United States of America can't do. So that decision from 1930 and 1940 is active today. You don't think Russia wants to be as good in microelectronics as we are, but they can't. You don't think United States wants to rely on Russia for rocket engines? We want to build them better, but we can't. They're just better than us. So what happens is you pick, as you go into a market, you pick a strategic competitive advantage and you focus on it. And today Russia dominates in metallurgy and their rocket engines and their jet fighters and the things that can build things better than we can. And we have better microelectronics and we can build better servers and we have Google. So. But the point is you can't channel hop. You can't just say, oh microelectronics turned out to be great. That was a good strategic choice. We're going to compete with you on that. So in our market we chose, we chose the analytics of where to mine as our competitive strength. And we do, and we invested in it more than other people did. You don't think other companies want to do it as well as we can? Too bad. They need to get in a time machine, go back 15 years when we started doing it, and invest the way we did. Okay? Back to the future. They can't. They cannot do it as well as us. We're the best at doing that. So that's important. So there's 10 of us, but we're the best. So we get the highest margin. So if you're an investor in our deal, we get the highest margin of any of the 10 competitors. Because we are more efficient at knowing where to apply our resources than the next guys. And it's very important because other people do what we do. Right? Now there's other people who are better at digging. We admit that. But we don't think digging is the dominant skill in this market. We think knowing where to dig. We're pretty damn good at digging, but there's guys who are amazing at it. And as you see me here today, there's guys who are amazing at selling. They suck at knowing where to dig. And they suck at digging because they buy old equipment and they don't apply technologies. Right? And they got lots of young guys running around. It's a cool place to work. But their skill is selling. They sell much harder than we do, but they don't make as much money. They just sell harder. Our skill is in knowing where to dig, and that's the dominant competitive advantage in the market. So the market is changing. There's an opportunity. There's a window of opportunity now to buy this up. We don't think that these forces are going to hold. We think other companies are going to get these technologies and learn how to apply them. We think the trend line is going to change and flatten out. All right? And we think that it's going to plateau this social interest that the economy gets better. The social interest to buy silver will flatten out as well. So there's a market window of opportunity now. You couldn't do this a year ago. You couldn't do this a year ago or two years ago because the technology wasn't as good. And the social desire for silver wasn't as strong. And you might not be able to buy the way we can do it now, two years from now, as these trend lines flatten out. Now is the market. Now is the window of opportunity. We're better than our competitors. The price is at an all-time high. The forces are in our direction. And if you were ever going to do this, it wasn't two years ago and it's not two years from now, now is the time to do it. I want to tell you a little bit about this. Business that I just learned about five minutes ago. I think I said to you, what we do, so we do this incredibly hard work of knowing where to dig, we apply the equipment, we get this stuff out of the mind, that's all the hard work. We send it off to Smelter. The reason we don't do that, right, that's a low margin business. It's easy to do. It requires very little technology and that, you know, we just outsource it. There's no value in having that. So we do the hard work where we can get a real competitive advantage. We find investors, we find investors who care about commodities, right? We find investors who care about commodities and maybe they're getting a 40 or 50% IRR in commodities, right? But how we find for our company that we know a guy is right is he's bought commodities and he may even bought gold, silver, or copper before and he's looking, he's looking for the appreciation bogey that we provide of an 80% return and he's okay to take the risk, right? Because every once in a while we go to dig and we miss and there's a risk. But the investor we're looking for that's perfect for us and in fact all of you, if you can't stomach the risk and you want an 8% return for your money, you should go somewhere else, right? This is an 80% plus yield with an appropriate amount of risk. But if you like that, you're perfect because you'll like us as people. We're fair and we're honest and we're integral and we're transparent and we give visibility and we have a long track record and I will work very hard at everything that I say I'm going to do. So we're good guys to work with, you take some risk with us that's appropriate, you get an 80% yield. But that's just perfect for us. If you're outside those bookends, you're not for us. You should go somewhere else and I'll tell you where else to go, right? You should go invest in Groupon or go buy a triplex in Ocean Beach with tenants in it and make a 3% yield a year but have a piece of real estate that you own, right? We find our investors, the way we go to market is at conferences. So there's conferences that care about mining and we go there and we make a presentation, right? And some people are interested in the presentation because they care about how we apply technology and they care about the gross margin that we get and they care about our track record over time and our ability to do this and so after we do a presentation at the conference, they come to us and they say, I love your presentation, you guys seem more of an expert than anyone else and then we open up a conversation with them and then we open up a relationship and that's how we go into the channel, that's how we find our investors, that's how we go to market. We make money for ourselves, you know, most of the money is made by the investor. We only make money for ourselves when the investor makes money. We give 80% of the return to the investor and we keep 20% for ourselves, right? So we capture part of the value that we give our investors, we capture it for ourselves but if our investors don't make money, we don't make a thing, okay? In addition, we co-invest, we co-invest, we take risk ourselves. So that's how we capture value, that's how we make money. So sometimes what will happen is I'll be pitching investor but our competitor will be pitching the same investor as well. And so people want the same investors and the same revenue streams that we're trying to get so we have to have ways to defend ourselves from those investors, right? And the main way we do that is with our technological ability, right? We explain to the investor, you can certainly go with that other guy, right? But we call what they do hunt and peck, hunt and peck and we have precision targeting, right? And we have our long track record of success because we apply precision targeting and it works. And that's how we get the high yield and that's how we protect our investors with precision targeting. So that's why I'd like you to invest in this business today. There are many other people investing, right? As you can see, it's not difficult with the level of technology and precision and accuracy and hard work and experience that we have for us to find people and we don't want bad investors. Investors aren't like customers, right? Because as William said, we're public, there's tremendous security oversight. So if you're a crappy investor, even if you give me the money, I suffer. So I really, really have to screen you hard. First of all, I have lots and lots of good investors over here that are already in. I don't want to introduce a bad investor to a good gene pool. So I'd like to hear from you right now what kind of investor you are and how you behave when you're in a deal and why we would like to take your money. So if you could reflect back to me, I think that would be a good place to take the conversation. And now I've given you the complete pitch for William's business. So I wanted to share that with you so you could see when you have the pitching skill, you know, there's a business I learned about and I'm sure I got some things wrong and I'm sorry about that. I'm not really sorry. Okay, so I can do things wrong. I'm never sorry. Why would I be? You know how much effort I'm not getting paid to do this? Why should I be sorry? Come here and give you guys all this value, share everything I know. I now know nothing. You guys know everything I know. If I'm going to speak to you again, I've got to find some new stuff. I've come here on a Saturday, left my family, spoken on stage, shared everything I know with you guys, given you all my intellectual property, shared with you as much as I can possibly do. It's not possible I could be sorry. I'm just happy I could do that for you. I am. Okay. Okay, so where do we go next? This thing, what I would like for you to do is reflect on the pitch that we gave here, right? And just to deconstruct it, we try to develop some intrigue about this business. You know, I just came up with this idea of us against the earth, its tension, its intrigue. Find some kind of narrative. Talk about the moving forces in the market. Why you couldn't do this a year ago and why it may be too risky to do a year from now. Opened up a market window, but I haven't said we're the only one who knows how to do this. I've said other guys are coming through the market window with us and we compete with them. But let me tell you how we compete. They do hunt and peck and we do precision and that's the difference between us. And then I described to you our business model and who it's for and who it's not for. So I think you can take those basic elements and continue to refine your pitch. Okay, so good. So we're going to move into the next segment. I think this was great and I do appreciate the energy that I feel back coming back from the room and the attention. Because this is the last thing I want to say. Your dream as a presenter, you walk into a room, you get on the phone, you get on the internet. What you want is attention. So as I tell my guys, if I could lock you down in a chair, pin your eyes open, pin your ears back, feed you coffee and inject you with whatever the government has to make people alert. I don't have to be that good. I can sit here for four hours. I can sell whatever it is. Blue plastic frogs, mining squirrel nut scratchers, rockets, birthday cakes. You'll buy it all because you have to pay attention. You start to lose attention, we inject you with some more stuff. What you want is attention. That's what you're trying to get. And what I'm hoping to say is, yes, it's easy to get attention by lifting the volume very high, but then you scare people. So the social tools to get and keep attention. And you guys have been giving me attention for a long time and that is something I do appreciate. You
